answers.field_label: Description Of Nature Purpose And Beneficiaries Of Project Line 1
answers.value: The 14th Avenue Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project will retrofit an existing approximately 1.2-mile segment of 14th Avenue, an established urban collector roadway, to improve pedestrian, bicycle, and traffic safety while maintaining existing vehicular capacity. The project includes installation of new ADA-compliant curb ramps at all intersections, high-visibility crosswalks, upgraded pedestrian signal heads with countdown timers, Class II buffered bike lanes in both directions achieved primarily through restriping within existing curb-to-curb width, and limited sidewalk infill where short gaps currently force pedestrians into the roadway.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Description Of Nature Purpose And Beneficiaries Of Project Line 2
answers.value: Additional elements include replacement of deteriorated pavement sections, minor drainage inlet adjustments, new street lighting at selected intersections to improve nighttime visibility, and installation of traffic-calming features such as curb extensions (bulb-outs) at key crosswalks adjacent to schools and neighborhood parks. All work is confined to previously disturbed and developed public right-of-way; no acquisition of new right-of-way is required, and no expansion of the roadway footprint beyond existing curbs will occur. Construction activities will be phased to maintain access to residences, local businesses, and emergency services at all times.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Description Of Nature Purpose And Beneficiaries Of Project Line 3
answers.value: The primary beneficiaries of the project are local residents of the Tahoe Park and Tallac Village neighborhoods, students and families walking and biking to nearby schools (including Will C. Wood Middle School and Tahoe Elementary School), transit riders accessing Sacramento Regional Transit bus routes that run along or cross 14th Avenue, and motorists traveling between Stockton Boulevard and 65th Street. By reducing vehicle speeds, shortening pedestrian crossing distances, and providing dedicated bicycle facilities, the project is intended to reduce the frequency and severity of collisions, encourage active transportation, and improve safe access to community resources such as parks, schools, small businesses, and medical offices along the corridor.

answers.field_label: Reasons Why Project Is Exempt Line 1
answers.value: The project consists of minor alterations to existing public streets, sidewalks, and related appurtenances involving negligible or no expansion of use beyond that existing at the time of the lead agency’s determination. All proposed improvements—including pavement rehabilitation, restriping to add buffered bike lanes within existing curb lines, installation of ADA-compliant curb ramps, crosswalk enhancements, minor drainage modifications, and lighting upgrades—occur entirely within the existing, fully improved public right-of-way along 14th Avenue. As such, the project is categorically exempt from CEQA under Class 1 (Existing Facilities), CEQA Guidelines §15301(c), which specifically includes existing streets, sidewalks, bicycle and pedestrian trails, and similar facilities.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Reasons Why Project Is Exempt Line 2
answers.value: The project area is fully urbanized, previously graded, and developed, and does not contain sensitive biological resources, prime farmland, wetlands, or designated scenic resources. The improvements do not increase the number of traffic lanes, do not induce substantial growth, and do not involve any unusual circumstances that would create a reasonable possibility of a significant effect on the environment. There are no known hazardous waste sites on or adjacent to the project corridor as listed pursuant to Government Code §65962.5, and the project will comply with all applicable City standard construction specifications, noise control ordinances, and best management practices for dust and stormwater. Therefore, none of the exceptions to the use of categorical exemptions set forth in CEQA Guidelines §15300.2 apply, and the Class 1 exemption is appropriate.

persona.background_context:
This is Daniel, a 39-year-old senior planner who lives with his wife and two kids in the Tahoe Park/Tallac Village area of Sacramento, not far from the stretch of 14th Avenue he works on professionally and drives through almost every day. He’s been with the City of Sacramento Department of Public Works for nearly a decade, and in the last three years he’s moved into a senior planner role in the Environmental Services Division of the Engineering and Project Delivery group. His work focuses on making neighborhood streets safer and more comfortable for people walking, biking, and taking transit while ensuring projects comply with environmental regulations. The 14th Avenue Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project is one of his current responsibilities, and he takes pride in the fact that it directly serves the Tahoe Park and Tallac Village neighborhoods his own family uses: his daughter Sofia walks to Tahoe Elementary School with friends, and his son Mateo rides his bike to Will C. Wood Middle School’s after-school soccer practices along nearby routes.

On a typical weekday, Daniel is up around 6:15 a.m., making coffee and packing lunches before the kids get ready for school. He and his wife Elena, who works part-time as a medical assistant at a clinic off Stockton Boulevard, coordinate drop-offs depending on who has early meetings or late shifts. Daniel usually bikes or occasionally drives the short commute from their 62nd Street home into downtown, heading to the Public Works offices at 915 I Street. His days are a mix of reviewing traffic safety data, coordinating with engineers on design tweaks for buffered bike lanes and curb extensions, and talking with community members and school staff about concerns near crosswalks and intersections. Because the 14th Avenue corridor is fully urbanized and already developed, he spends time making sure pavement rehabilitation, new ADA ramps, lighting upgrades, and drainage adjustments all fit within existing curbs and right-of-way and don’t create unexpected impacts for nearby residents or small businesses.

Outside of work, most of Daniel’s life revolves around family and neighborhood routines. Weeknights alternate between helping Sofia with her math homework, driving Mateo to soccer practice at a nearby park, and squeezing in a quick jog through Tahoe Park before dark. His parents, Carlos and Rosa, live about fifteen minutes away in South Sacramento and often come over on Sundays for dinner; Rosa insists on bringing homemade tamales or caldo, and Carlos likes to talk with Daniel about how much the neighborhood has changed since he first moved there in the 1980s. Recently, Daniel and Elena have been talking about whether to replace their aging second car or keep relying more on bikes and transit, especially with rising costs and their hope to start a modest college fund for the kids. He’s also keeping an eye on Elena’s workload at the clinic, as she’s been feeling worn down after a winter of back-to-back respiratory cases, and they’re trying to be diligent about carving out one quiet evening a week with no activities, just a simple dinner and a movie at home.

Over the past year, Daniel has felt both satisfaction and pressure at work. He’s proud that neighborhood parents and local principals support the safety improvements along 14th Avenue, especially the new crosswalks and lighting near schools and parks, but he also knows construction will bring temporary noise, dust, and detours. He’s been meeting with colleagues in traffic engineering and stormwater to make sure construction phases maintain access for residents, businesses, and emergency services along the corridor. He worries a bit about community frustration when crews start tearing up the pavement, so he’s been encouraging more outreach at PTA meetings and neighborhood association gatherings. At home, his main concerns are more personal and immediate: keeping up with the mortgage on their modest house, trying to stay healthy after a minor bout of high blood pressure flagged at his last physical in October 2025, and finding enough time to be present with Sofia and Mateo as they grow more independent. Despite the juggling act, Daniel feels rooted in Sacramento and in his role helping make everyday streets safer for the same families, neighbors, and kids he sees on his own block.

form_summary.form_purpose: Notice of Exemption form used by a public agency to document and file that a specific project is exempt from environmental review requirements under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA). It records basic project information, the exemption type, and the reasons for the exemption.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Staff at the California Office of Planning and Research and the relevant County Clerk, who process and file CEQA exemption notices for public record and compliance tracking; the form is prepared and signed by the lead public agency or project applicant, not by the general public.

artifacts.content:
Elena,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m staring at this stack of collision maps again. I keep thinking about how much of our life is wrapped up in these few streets.

Every time I bike Mateo to his Saturday soccer at the Tahoe Park fields, that stretch by the 7‑Eleven at 14th & Stockton still makes my stomach clench. I can’t get the March 2022 almost‑dooring out of my head—one car door a second earlier and I’d have been on the pavement with him watching. It’s part of why I pushed so hard for those buffered bike lanes on 14th. We’re still a one‑car household after everything with your schedule and Dad’s medical bills; that corridor is basically our family’s spine.

And then there’s all the kid stuff on top of it. Sofia still tenses up walking near 14th & 63rd after that March 8, 2023 near‑miss on her way to Tahoe Elementary. I know I tell people at work the project is about “neighborhood safety,” but inside it’s about *our* kids, our block at 4125 62nd, Mom’s fall at 60th, Luis Hernandez getting killed at 60th back in November 2021—this whole corridor is a scrapbook of close calls and one tragedy too many.

I also keep replaying that May 2, 2022 road‑rage incident at 14th & 58th when that driver screamed at you and swerved toward you and Mateo. No wonder you insist on walking him to Tahoe at 7:45 every morning.

Sorry, this got heavy. Tomorrow let’s just bike to Maple Park if you’re up for it after your shift and try to remember why we’re fighting so hard to fix these streets.

– D

metadata.subject: Why this corridor won’t leave my head

artifacts.content:
Mariana,

I’ve been meaning to write a real update instead of those quick texts.

First, your mom’s caldo on Sunday was exactly what we all needed. Mine’s been struggling more since her Type 2 diabetes diagnosis at Kaiser South Sacramento last January. She’s trying to be good about walking—30–40 minutes along 14th Ave to the Kaiser clinic three times a week—but I hate how stressed she looks at some of those crossings. She told me last week she almost turned around at 60th because the traffic felt too fast.

It’s wild how much of our family history is tied to busted concrete. Remember when she fell and fractured her wrist at the broken curb ramp at 14th & 60th back on 11/3/21, trying to get to the bus for that Kaiser appointment? That’s burned into my brain every time I review ADA ramp details. Same with Dad almost going down twice last winter stepping into my car in front of 4125 because of the ponding and broken pavement—this, after his knee surgery at Sutter in Feb 2021.

I know I sound like a walking CEQA memo, but honestly, the “minor drainage inlet adjustments” and curb extensions we’re doing are really about Mom and Dad and the other abuelos on our block. When I say the project is for “seniors and people with mobility issues,” I’m picturing Mom with her cane and glucose meter.

Anyway, enough planner ranting. How are you holding up with your shifts and Miguel’s recovery after his crash near Mercy General? Let’s plan a low‑key dinner at our place in a couple weeks—no tamales arms race between the moms this time, just something simple.

Un abrazo,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Mom, Dad, and all these sidewalks

artifacts.content:
Javi,

Catching you up on the not‑so‑Instagrammable parts of life.

Work: I finally got the 14th Ave safety project locked in as a Class 1 “existing facilities” rehab—pavement, striping, ADA ramps, lighting, all inside the current curb lines. After the City Attorney’s June 2022 closed‑session warning about how any perceived capacity increase could complicate Luis Herrera’s ADA lawsuit over his fall at the bad curb ramp by 62nd, I’ve been almost paranoid about staying within §15301(c).

That paranoia isn’t totally abstract either. I’m still carrying baggage from the 2019 internal audit that nailed me on the Broadway restriping hazmat oversight and landed that written PIP in my HR file through June 2020. Then there was the Franklin Blvd CEQA schedule I blew, the May 2023 performance plan, Karen Lee’s “we do not need an EIR on this one” talk in August, and Kevin Liu’s anonymous August 2023 complaint accusing me of rubber‑stamping exemptions while I was on FMLA for stress after Mateo’s behavioral suspension from Mark Twain Elementary. It’s…a lot.

On top of that, I met with the City Attorney, Lisa Hernandez, on Dec 12, 2024 about the whistleblower retaliation grievance I filed in October when a former deputy director tried to get me to “find a way” around an EIR on that infill project. I literally walked out of her office, went to my 3rd‑floor cubicle, and ran EnviroStor checks on every parcel in the new Class 1 corridors just so no one can say I cut corners.

Home is the only place that feels sane: Elena’s hanging in there after the license mess, Sofia’s obsessed with fractions, and Mateo’s back on the soccer field instead of the principal’s office. Come over for carne asada soon; I’ll trade you CEQA war stories for baby pictures.

– D

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – why these projects feel so loaded lately

I keep telling people the 14th Ave work is “routine rehab,” but there’s nothing routine about how it sits in my gut.

I still see Sofia in that crosswalk at 14th & 58th on the morning of 9/14/23, when the car blew through and nearly sideswiped her. A year before that, Diego from down the block actually *was* hit there in May 2022 and ended up at UC Davis Med Center. Add Luis Hernandez’s fatal crash at 14th & 60th on 11/4/21, and it feels like every block of that corridor has a ghost.

Mom’s story is literally written into the concrete too: the fall and fractured wrist at the busted curb ramp at 14th & 60th on 11/3/21, then her diabetes diagnosis at Kaiser South Sac in Jan 2023 and those 30–40 minute walks along 14th to the clinic three times a week. Dad with his cane after the Sutter knee surgery in Feb 2021, almost slipping twice at the curb in front of 4125 because of ponding.

Then there’s our own choices: going down to one car in July 2022 after Dad’s stroke at Methodist and the $18k in medical/caregiving costs; Elena’s March 2023 DUI and six‑month license suspension that turned 14th Ave into our family’s lifeline on foot to Food Source and the clinic at Stockton. All of that is why buffered bike lanes, curb extensions, and crosswalks don’t feel abstract.

Note to self: remember these stories when the design meetings get bogged down in lane widths and MUTCD citations. This is about making sure my kids, my parents, and my neighbors can get through a day without ending up in an ER—or a courtroom.

From: Daniel Morales <dmorales@sacpublicworks.gov>
To: Elena Ruiz Morales <elenaruiz@example.com>
Date: March 12, 2024 9:14 PM
Subject: Why these school projects feel so personal

Elena,

I’m finishing up the narrative for the Safe Routes and 14th Ave packages tonight and just needed to write this out to you. On paper it’s all “traffic-calming” and “CEQA Class 1 – Existing Facilities,” but in my head it’s Sofia, Mateo, and our block.

For the Safe Routes to School project on the 62nd Street corridor and Franklin Boulevard by Ethel I. Baker Elementary School, I keep going back to March 3, 2023. That near-collision at the crosswalk in front of Ethel I. Baker, when the speeding driver blew through and came within a few feet of hitting Sofia as she crossed with her classmates, is still burned into me. The curb extensions, new LED streetlights, and high‑visibility crosswalks we’re proposing along 62nd are really about protecting kids like ours who walk and bike there every weekday during the 2023–2024 school year—Sofia at 10 and Mateo at 7.

Same with the 14th Avenue Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project. I framed it as a Class 1 exemption under CEQA Guidelines §15301(c) because it’s just resurfacing, restriping, lighting upgrades, and ADA‑compliant curb ramps within the existing right‑of‑way along 14th Avenue. But what I’m really thinking about is Sofia’s wheelchair‑accessible school bus struggling to safely load and unload near Oak Ridge Elementary since her March 2023 spinal surgery at UC Davis Medical Center.

On the forms I talk about “vulnerable users.” Privately, I’m just trying to keep our kids—and kids like them—safe.

– D

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Morales <dmorales@sacpublicworks.gov>
To: 14th Ave Project File <14thAve_ProjectNotes@cityofsac.gov>
Date: January 29, 2024 7:02 AM
Subject: Personal background for internal context – 14th Ave / 62nd St (DO NOT DISTRIBUTE)

Team,

Adding some personal context here strictly for internal understanding of why I’ve been so adamant about the 14th Avenue and 62nd Street safety scope. Please do NOT include this verbatim in any public documents.

Along 14th Avenue near Oak Park and Tallac Village, the Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project became urgent for me after two incidents at 14th Avenue and 58th Street. On the morning of September 14, 2023, my daughter, Sofia Morales, was nearly sideswiped in the crosswalk there—a near-miss sideswipe in the crosswalk by a speeding driver. Earlier, in May 2022, my neighbor’s son Diego was actually hit at that same location, a pedestrian collision with a speeding driver that ended with emergency medical treatment at UC Davis Medical Center. Those events are why I pushed within the City of Sacramento Department of Public Works to prioritize this 1.2‑mile segment so families in our Oak Park and Tallac Village neighborhoods, especially kids walking to Ethel I. Baker Elementary School and nearby churches, aren’t facing these kinds of close calls and injuries.

Separately, on the 62nd Street residential segment by my home at 4125 62nd Street, chronic speeding and cut‑through traffic have led to multiple near‑misses. In May 2022, my mother, Rosa Morales, was almost struck while unloading my son Mateo from our car in front of our single‑family residence frontage. The traffic‑calming elements we’re proposing—curb extensions and improved lighting at intersections near our block—are intended to protect elderly residents like her and families with young children who regularly park and cross there.

– Daniel Christopher Morales, Senior Planner, Environmental Services

1. Confirm date Notice of Exemption (NOE) was **received for filing at OPR** for the 14th Avenue Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project.
2. Clarify any discrepancies between City Clerk transmittal date (2/19/2026) and OPR intake log (Jenna mentioned they show **2/21/2026** as the initial receipt date).
3. Verify that the project qualifies under the Class 1 categorical exemption and that no CEQA Guidelines §150300.2 exceptions are triggered.

Notes to self:
- Bring printout of DTSC EnviroStor search results I ran on December 12, 2024 at my cubicle on the 3rd floor after meeting with Lisa about the whistleblower retaliation grievance I filed in October 2024 regarding pressure from a former deputy director to “find a way” to avoid an EIR on that politically sensitive infill project. This shows there are no hazardous waste sites along the 14th Avenue corridor.
- Highlight that the project consists of minor alterations to existing public infrastructure in a fully urbanized setting, with no expansion of capacity and no increase in traffic lanes.
- Ask Jenna to email written confirmation of the OPR received date so we can attach it to the CEQA admin record.

Reminder: Block off 15 minutes after the call to update the internal CEQA tracking spreadsheet with the OPR filing date Jenna confirms.

Our internal log shows that your Notice of Exemption packet was stamped “RECEIVED – CLERK/RECORDER” on 02/18/2026 at 4:07 PM when it was dropped off at the public counter. artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Morales <daniel.morales@cityofsacramento.gov>
To: CEQA Intake <state.clearinghouse@opr.ca.gov>
Cc: amy.hsu@cityofsacramento.gov; mark.dunlevy@cityofsacramento.gov
Date: 2026-02-21 9:18 AM
Subject: Clarification on OPR Received Date – 14th Avenue NOE

Dear OPR Staff,

I’m writing to clarify how we should reference the “date received for filing at OPR” in our internal tracking for the 14th Avenue Complete Streets and Safety Improvements Project (City Project No. PW-14AV-24).

Our Sacramento County Clerk/Recorder stamped the Notice of Exemption as received on 02/18/2026, and their office indicates they transmitted the NOE to you on 02/20/2026 as part of a batch. For our capital program schedule, we’ve been tentatively noting 02/21/2026 as the operative date for the CEQA posting window, assuming that is when your system will show the document as received.

Because this project qualifies for a Class 1 exemption (CEQA Guidelines §15301(c)) and consists solely of minor alterations to existing streets, sidewalks, and related appurtenances along 14th Avenue with no expansion of capacity, the exact received date matters for when we can authorize construction advertising.

We’re under some pressure due to budget and personal factors: my family went down to one car in July 2022 after my father, Carlos Morales, had a stroke at Methodist Hospital of Sacramento and we took on significant medical and caregiving expenses, so I’m keenly aware of how important safer walking and biking conditions are for families relying on active transportation.

Could you please confirm which date you will list as the official OPR received date for this filing so I can align our internal forms?

Sincerely,

Daniel Christopher Morales
Senior Planner, Environmental Services
City of Sacramento Department of Public Works
